metadata.title: Evidence Updates Beliefs, It Doesn't Replace Them
metadata.color_semantics: TEAL = Bayesian updating / base rate / correct posterior; CRIMSON = base rate neglect / representativeness / wrong intuition
metadata.note: Exclusions honored: no Linda conjunction fallacy, no formal Bayes derivation, no frequentist debate. Wonder register.
_variant_todo: rewrite every beat narration_text in the Teardown register (voices/teardown/VOICE.md + CLAUDE-BRAND.md) — voice only, facts unchanged
signature tangent 0-1 per video, ONLY on a clear opportunity (see SKILL)
swap the outro to the Claude outro from AUTHOR.MD :: NikBearBrown
then build audience-namespaced (audio in the new voice, scenes in the new palette)

skin_warnings: B00: palette=claude but the cold open is 'un-annotated' — COLD OPEN LAW wants ClaudeComposerAsk
B11: palette=claude but the outro is 'un-annotated' — OUTRO LAW wants ClaudeTitleOutro

beats.narration_text: This is Liam, in for Bear. med davy A-I. From Sanskrit — meaning intelligent, or intellectually brilliant. The perfect name for our A-I-powered intelligent learning systems.

props.headline: Medhavy AI
props.body:
मेधावी (Medhavy): From Sanskrit, meaning
"intelligent" or "intellectually brilliant"
— the perfect name for our AI-powered
intelligent learning systems.

beats.narration_text: Steve is meek, tidy, passionate about order and detail. Is he a librarian or a farmer? Most people say librarian. But for every librarian there are 20 farmers — and Steve's description matches a lot of farmers too. Evidence updates belief; it doesn't replace the base rate.

card.copy: Evidence Updates Beliefs, It Doesn't Replace Them

build.suggested: suggested prompt: Steve is meek, tidy, passionate about order and detail. Is he a librarian or a farmer? Most people say librarian. But for every librarian there are 20 farmers —

beats.narration_text: Bayes' theorem: posterior probability equals prior times likelihood ratio. The prior is the base rate. The likelihood ratio is how much more probable the evidence is under one hypothesis versus the other.

production_viz.label: P(H|E) = P(H) × likelihood ratio equation; prior base rate and likelihood ratio labeled separately; TEAL for correct framework

beats.narration_text: Steve's description fits the librarian stereotype perfectly. Ignoring the base rate gives about 80 percent confidence he is a librarian. With the base rate: 17 percent. Why is ignoring the base rate so systematically wrong?

card.copy: Perfect librarian description. Ignoring base rate: 80% confident librarian. With base rate: 17%. What is the base rate doing?
card.sub: The base rate overwhelms the description

build.suggested: suggested prompt: Steve's description fits the librarian stereotype perfectly. Ignoring the base rate gives about 80 percent confidence he is a librarian. With the base rate: 17

beats.narration_text: In any representative sample: 10 librarians for every 200 farmers. Of 10 librarians, 40 percent match Steve's description — 4 people. Of 200 farmers, 10 percent match — 20 people. Among 24 who match, 4 are librarians: 4 divided by 24 equals 17 percent.

production_viz.label: Crowd: 10 librarians + 200 farmers; matching ones step forward: 4 librarians + 20 farmers; 4/24 = 17% labeled; TEAL for librarians, CRIMSON for farmers

beats.narration_text: The description is genuinely more common among librarians: 40 percent versus 10 percent. That ratio is the likelihood ratio: 4 to 1. But the prior ratio is 10 librarians to 200 farmers — 1 to 20. The prior overwhelms the likelihood.

production_viz.label: Likelihood ratio 4:1 (TEAL for favoring librarian) vs prior odds 1:20 (CRIMSON for favoring farmer); prior overwhelms likelihood shown

beats.narration_text: Kahneman and Tversky called this representativeness heuristic: people judge probability by how well an example matches a stereotype, ignoring how common that stereotype is in the population.

production_viz.label: Representativeness heuristic diagram: stereotype match (CRIMSON, dominant in intuition) vs base rate (TEAL, ignored); Kahneman/Tversky label

beats.narration_text: Bayes in odds form: posterior odds equals prior odds times likelihood ratio. Prior odds: 1 to 20. Likelihood ratio: 4 to 1. Posterior odds: 4 to 20 equals 1 to 5. Probability of librarian: approximately 17 percent.

production_viz.label: Odds calculation: 1:20 × 4:1 = 4:20 = 1:5 → 17%; step by step in TEAL; wrong intuitive 80% in CRIMSON for comparison

beats.narration_text: If base rates were equal — 10 librarians and 10 farmers — the likelihood ratio would dominate: 40 percent versus 10 percent, librarian wins easily. The prior matters as much as the evidence. When they conflict, neither automatically wins.

production_viz.label: Equal priors scenario: 10 lib + 10 farmers; 4/6 = 67% librarian; TEAL showing prior matters equally to evidence

beats.narration_text: This error affects real decisions. Doctors matching symptoms to a diagnosis without weighing how rare that diagnosis is commit the same error. Bayes' theorem is the correct framework for updating beliefs — not replacing them.

production_viz.label: Doctor scenario: rare diagnosis (low base rate CRIMSON) vs symptom match (high likelihood ratio TEAL); correct Bayesian balance needed

beats.narration_text: P(H|E) equals P(H) times P(E|H) over P(E). Steve: prior 1 to 20 librarian-to-farmer. Likelihood ratio 4 to 1. Posterior approximately 1 to 5, 17 percent librarian. Most say 80 percent. Base rate neglect is the error.

card.copy: Prior: 1 librarian per 20 farmers. Likelihood ratio: 40%/10% = 4:1. Posterior = 4:20 = 17%. Most say 80%. Representativeness heuristic ignores the base rate.
card.sub: Evidence updates beliefs, not replaces them

build.suggested: suggested prompt: P(H|E) equals P(H) times P(E|H) over P(E). Steve: prior 1 to 20 librarian-to-farmer. Likelihood ratio 4 to 1. Posterior approximately 1 to 5, 17 percent librari

beats.narration_text: med davy. A-I-powered intelligent learning systems. On YouTube, at med davy A-I. med davy dot com.
